params.text:
The law behind speaking up

The Public Interest Disclosure Act 1998 (PIDA) gives legal protection to workers who blow the whistle. When your concern meets the conditions in the law it becomes a protected disclosure. To be protected, you must:

  • disclose information you reasonably believe shows a qualifying wrongdoing (you do not have to be proved right — an honest, reasonable belief is enough); and
  • reasonably believe the disclosure is in the public interest; and
  • raise it through an appropriate route, such as your employer or a prescribed person.

These are “day one” rights — you are covered from your very first day at work, with no minimum length of service required.

params.alt: Speaking up in a team meeting

params.text:
Categories of qualifying wrongdoing

PIDA sets out the types of wrongdoing that a protected disclosure can be about. Your concern should fit one of these categories:

  • A criminal offence — for example theft, fraud or physical assault.
  • A breach of a legal obligation — failing to meet a duty set out in law or regulation.
  • A danger to health and safety of any person.
  • A miscarriage of justice.
  • Damage to the environment.
  • The deliberate concealment (cover-up) of any of the above.

In social care, abuse and neglect will usually fall within a criminal offence, a breach of a legal obligation, or a danger to health and safety.

params.taskDescription: Complete the summary of what makes a disclosure protected under PIDA.
params.textField: A protected disclosure must be about a *qualifying* wrongdoing. The worker only needs a *reasonable* belief that the information is true, and must believe the disclosure is in the *public* interest. Protection applies from the worker's first *day*, with no minimum length of *service* required.
